I am trying to compile numpy 2.1.1 from source on a platform with intel ifort/icc/icpc compilers, i.e., not the newer oneapi icx/ifx/icpx compilers. I know this is no longer supported by intel, but I've compiled previous versions of numpy with the older intel compilers before, so I thought I would give it a try. However, the compilation barfs as below. I am pasting a few instances of the error, which occurs on multiple source files. If it's true that numpy 2.1.1 can no longer be compiled with ifort/icc/icpc, then what is the last version of numpy that could be compiled with those?

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
CC=icc CXX=icpc FC=ifort F77=ifort CFLAGS='-xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict' FFLAGS='-xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict' CXXFLAGS='-xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict -std=c++17' python -m pip install --no-build-isolation -v . -Cbuild-dir=build -Csetup-args=-Dallow-noblas=false -Csetup-args=-Dblas-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dlapack-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -Csetup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp
```


### Error message:

```shell
In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_import.h(7),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.h(12),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/buffer.c(16):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_atomic.h(37): error: expression must have pointer-to-struct-or-union type
        return (npy_uint8)atomic_load((const _Atomic(uint8_t)*)obj);
                          ^

  In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_import.h(7),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.h(12),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/buffer.c(16):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_atomic.h(52): error: expression must have pointer-to-struct-or-union type
        return atomic_load((const _Atomic(void *)*)obj);
               ^

  In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_import.h(7),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.h(12),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/buffer.c(16):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_atomic.h(75): error: expression must have pointer-to-struct-or-union type
        atomic_store((_Atomic(uint8_t)*)obj, value);
        ^

  In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_import.h(7),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.h(12),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/multiarray/buffer.c(16):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/npy_atomic.h(87): error: expression must have pointer-to-struct-or-union type
        atomic_store((_Atomic(void *)*)obj, value);
        ^
```

Python 3.11.10

Here's the configure line I used to compile python 3.11.10, and you can see the `CFLAGS` etc (plus some custom paths for my platform):
```
./configure --prefix=/discover/nobackup/projects/tm5var4d/packages/python/3.11.10 --enable-optimizations --with-computed-gotos --with-ensurepip=install --disable-ipv6 --with-libm=-limf \
    LIBS='-lpthread -limf -lirc' \
    CFLAGS='-O3 -g -fPIC -fp-model=strict -fwrapv -fomit-frame-pointer -xHost' \
    CC=icc CXX=icpc \
    CXXFLAGS='-O3 -g -fPIC -fp-model=strict -fwrapv -fomit-frame-pointer -xHost' \
    CPPFLAGS='-I/discover/nobackup/projects/tm5var4d/packages/openssl/1.1.1w/include -I/usr/include/ncursesw -I/discover/nobackup/projects/tm5var4d/packages/libffi/3.2.1/include' \
    LDFLAGS='-L/discover/nobackup/projects/tm5var4d/packages/openssl/1.1.1w/lib -L/discover/nobackup/projects/tm5var4d/packages/libffi/3.2.1/lib/../lib64'
```
Since the numpy build process outputs a lot of stuff besides those errors, it's possible that there is something there that will provide a clue as to what exactly is going wrong. I invoked the numpy build with
```
CC=icc CXX=icpc FC=ifort F77=ifort \
    CFLAGS='-xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict' \
    FFLAGS='-xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict' \
    CXXFLAGS='-xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict -std=c++17' \
    python -m pip install --no-build-isolation -v . -Cbuild-dir=build -Csetup-args=-Dallow-noblas=false \
    -Csetup-args=-Dblas-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dlapack-order=mkl \
    -Csetup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -Csetup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp
```
The first error that it threw was the following
```
  In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/common.hpp(7),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/npymath/halffloat.cpp(12):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/npdef.hpp(5): error: #error directive: "NumPy requires a compiler with at least C++17 enabled"
        #error "NumPy requires a compiler with at least C++17 enabled"
         ^
```
This was despite the `-std=c++17` that I added to `CXXFLAGS`. Reading up online, it seemed that `icc/icpc` needed a recent `gcc` to be present as well. So then I added `gcc 13.1.0` to the environment, after which the numpy build failed with
```
  [72/578] Compiling C++ object numpy/_core/libhighway.a.p/src_highway_hwy_abort.cc.o
  FAILED: numpy/_core/libhighway.a.p/src_highway_hwy_abort.cc.o
  icpc -Inumpy/_core/libhighway.a.p -Inumpy/_core -I../numpy/_core -I../numpy/_core/src/highway -I/gpfsm/dtse01/tdirs/batch/slurm.39336633.sbasu1/numpy-2.1.1/build/meson_cpu -fvisibility=hidden -DNDEBUG -D_FILE_OFFSET_BITS=64 -Wall -w3 -Wpch-messages -diag-disable:remark -std=c++17 -O3 -msse -msse2 -msse3 -DNPY_HAVE_SSE2 -DNPY_HAVE_SSE -DNPY_HAVE_SSE3 -xHost -fPIC -fp-model=strict -std=c++17 -fPIC -DTOOLCHAIN_MISS_ASM_HWCAP_H -MD -MQ numpy/_core/libhighway.a.p/src_highway_hwy_abort.cc.o -MF numpy/_core/libhighway.a.p/src_highway_hwy_abort.cc.o.d -o numpy/_core/libhighway.a.p/src_highway_hwy_abort.cc.o -c ../numpy/_core/src/highway/hwy/abort.cc
  In file included from /gpfsm/dulocal/sles12/other/gcc/13.1.0/bin/../include/c++/13.1.0/bits/char_traits.h(50),
                   from /gpfsm/dulocal/sles12/other/gcc/13.1.0/bin/../include/c++/13.1.0/string(42),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/highway/hwy/abort.cc(12):
  /gpfsm/dulocal/sles12/other/gcc/13.1.0/bin/../include/c++/13.1.0/type_traits(3363): error: type name is not allowed
      inline constexpr bool is_convertible_v = __is_convertible(_From, _To);
                                                                ^
```
The full build log is [here](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1kGXy6YP7MCdH0l5S0e9tptHs73D7Hy4o/view?usp=sharing).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2391573556/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Depending on how old the compiler is, you might be running a version from when they were still implementing constexpr.

* I tried 2021.7.0, the latest available on that system, and numpy compiled (surprisingly), but `numpy.test()` crashed on `_core/tests/test_einsum.py`.
* Next, I tried 2021.6.0, and `numpy` failed to build with
```
  In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/common.hpp(9),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/npysort/x86_simd_qsort.hpp(4),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/npysort/quicksort.cpp(57):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/npstd.hpp(34): error: namespace "std" has no member "complex"
    using std::complex;
               ^
```
* Compilers 2021.5.0 and 2021.4.0 failed with the same error as 2021.6.0.
* Then I tried 19.1.0.166, which failed with
```
  In file included from ../numpy/_core/src/common/common.hpp(10),
                   from ../numpy/_core/src/npymath/halffloat.cpp(12):
  ../numpy/_core/src/common/half.hpp(45): error: expected a ")"
        );
         ^
```
* Which brought me to 19.1.3.304, then 19.1.2.254, both of which failed with the same error as 19.1.0.166.

And that's the summary of all the compiler versions I have available on the system.

> I don't think you've mentioned the issue you originally ran into about use of C11 atomics, but now that I understand that that Python compiles successfully - it would help to see how it's compiling the pyatomic API - in particular which "flavor" of atomics its detecting.
> 
> In particular, it would help to know which branch of this preprocessor macro you end up in:
> 
> https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/d20c43d8e270016d743af6e8e0f8267e396a71f4/Include/cpython/pyatomic.h#L520-L544
> 
> If it picks C11 atomics, then I'm very confused why you're not hitting identical compiler errors from your original report, since the code should be identical.

The original issue I reported, `type name is not allowed`, went away when I switched from gcc 13 to gcc 12 following @r-devulap's suggestion.

> Finally, if supporting the Intel compilers is important to you, then you might be interested in improving NumPy's CI coverage to include a test run under a version of the Intel compilers, it's that's possible given licensing concerns. The reason this regressed is that we don't test with the Intel compilers and we don't test with the Intel compilers because no one has done the work to set that up. I don't think it's reasonable to expect something to continue to work if it isn't tested, so the only way to ensure that something you need continues to work is to verify that an upstream like NumPy is doing testing, or do the testing yourself and report bugs as they happen so they can be caught quickly.

I have to maintain and run a python stack on five different systems. Three of them have moved away from "classic" intel compilers to a recent (2023+) version of intel oneapi which actually builds numpy/scipy successfully. However, the other two still run classic intel compilers, so yes, supporting numpy on those platforms is important to me. The trials I'm encountering now is because of my effort to update the python/numpy stack on one of them, and going by my experience I'm in no hurry to attempt this on the other system.

How can I help add the classic intel compilers to numpy's CI coverage? If it involves periodically checking out code, building and reporting back errors or success status, that would be possible. If it involves giving someone else login access to these systems, that would be hard/impossible.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2408101594/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

I installed scipy from source with intel compilers as follows:
```

```
However, `scipy.test()` fails with the error in the title of this report.

```python
python -c 'import scipy; scipy.test()'

Describe the issue:

I compiled numpy from source with intel oneapi 2024.2. It built fine, but when executing `numpy.test()` there is one failure. I compiled numpy with 

```
CC=icx CXX=icpx FC=ifx CFLAGS='-fveclib=none -fp-model=strict -fPIC -xHost' FFLAGS='-fp-model=strict -fPIC -xHost' CXXFLAGS='-fp-model=strict -fPIC -xHost' python -m pip install --no-build-isolation -v . -Cbuild-dir=build -Csetup-args=-Dallow-noblas=false -Csetup-args=-Dblas-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dlapack-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -Csetup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp
```

i.e., I did ask for strict floating point, otherwise fast math can cause issues with numpy.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
python -c 'import numpy; numpy.test()'
```

Describe the issue:

```

```

i.e., I did ask for strict floating point, otherwise fast math can cause issues with numpy.

I compiled numpy from source with intel oneapi 2024.2. It built fine, but when executing `numpy.test()` there is one failure. I compiled numpy with 

```

```

i.e., I did ask for strict floating point, otherwise fast math can cause issues with numpy.

```python
python -c 'import numpy; numpy.test()'
```

NumPy's policy is to raise an invalid when it generates a NAN. But if you don't care for it, then this might not be worth pursuing. These things are hard to get right for all compiler/platform configurations.

OK, then I'm going to close this bug. On the compute environments I manage, the users typically don't rely on numpy to inform them about NANs, at least they're not supposed to. Instead, they're encouraged to explicitly check with `numpy.isnan`, `numpy.isinf`, `numpy.isfinite` etc. I agree that getting all floating point exception handling to be consistent across platforms and compilers is difficult.

However, the build fails with a lot of error messages that say "cannot overload a member function without a ref-qualifier with a member function with ref-qualifier". So whatever failed was due to the interaction between the updated pythran and updated oneapi compilers. 

I then tried going back to oneapi 2024.1, numpy 2.0.1, scipy 1.14.0, but downgraded pythran to 0.14.0. That failed with the same errors as above, `error: cannot overload a member function without a ref-qualifier with a member function with ref-qualifier '&&'`.

Finally, I tried the workaround of @rgommers, disabling the use of pythran altogether. That worked! It also reminded me that I had encountered this problem before, almost a year ago, trying to build recent scipy with intel compilers using the distutils route. The solution back then was to disable the use of pythran with `export SCIPY_USE_PYTHRAN=0`. Given how fickle the interaction of oneapi with pythran is, perhaps this should be one of the first solutions proposed in the docs somewhere for people trying to build scipy with oneapi+pythran?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2275984030/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

I am responsible for building python, numpy and scipy on a compute cluster that has intel oneapi compilers and MKL libraries. I built the latest scipy source from github with `CFLAGS='-fveclib=none' CC=icx CXX=icpx FC=ifx python dev.py build -C-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Duse-pythran=false`. It builds successfully, but `python dev.py test` fails with a segmentation fault.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
CFLAGS='-fveclib=none' CC=icx CXX=icpx FC=ifx python dev.py build -C-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Duse-pythran=false
python dev.py test
```

Describe your issue.

I am responsible for building python, numpy and scipy on a compute cluster that has intel oneapi compilers and MKL libraries. I built the latest scipy source from github with `CFLAGS='-fveclib=none' CC=icx CXX=icpx FC=ifx python dev.py build -C-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Duse-pythran=false`. It builds successfully, but `python dev.py test` fails with a segmentation fault.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
CFLAGS='-fveclib=none' CC=icx CXX=icpx FC=ifx python dev.py build -C-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Duse-pythran=false
python dev.py test
```

I have compiled python 3.12.4 and numpy 2.0.1 with intel oneapi 2024.1 compilers on a compute cluster for multiple people to use. I have some f2py code that explicitly uses openmp library calls such as `omp_set_num_threads`. With previous versions of python, including 3.11.6, I could compile them with 
```
f2py --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem -c interpolate_levels.pyf --opt='-qopenmp' -liomp5 interpolate_levels.F90
```
and they would produce perfectly working python modules. However, since everything has moved to meson now, this is no longer possible on python 3.12.4. So instead I did
```
FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c interpolate_levels.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 interpolate_levels.F90
```
which raised errors of `undefined reference to 'omp_set_num_threads'`. This should not happen because I am supplying `-qopenmp` as a compiler option.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c interpolate_levels.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 interpolate_levels.F90
```

I have compiled python 3.12.4 and numpy 2.0.1 with intel oneapi 2024.1 compilers on a compute cluster for multiple people to use. I have some f2py code that explicitly uses openmp library calls such as `omp_set_num_threads`. With previous versions of python, including 3.11.6, I could compile them with 
```
f2py --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem -c interpolate_levels.pyf --opt='-qopenmp' -liomp5 interpolate_levels.F90
```
and they would produce perfectly working python modules. However, since everything has moved to meson now, this is no longer possible on python 3.12.4. So instead I did
```
FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c interpolate_levels.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 interpolate_levels.F90
```
which raised errors of `undefined reference to 'omp_set_num_threads'`. This should not happen because I am supplying `-qopenmp` as a compiler option.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c interpolate_levels.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 interpolate_levels.F90
```

Put the following in `mwe_f2py.F90`,
```
module precon

implicit none

public :: xc_to_x

contains

subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)

   use omp_lib

   integer, intent(in)             :: n_state, num_threads
   double precision, intent(in)    :: G_state(n_state)
   double precision, intent(out)   :: x(n_state)
   integer                         :: i

   x = 0.0

   call omp_set_num_threads(num_threads)

   !$omp parallel do schedule(guided)
   do i = 1, n_state
      x(i) = G_state(i) * G_state(n_state-i+1)
   end do
   !$omp end parallel do

end subroutine xc_to_x

end module precon
```
and the following in `mwe_f2py.pyf`,
```
python module mwe_f2py
    interface

    module precon

        subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)
            integer,optional,intent(in),check(len(G_state)==n_state),depend(G_state) :: n_state=len(G_state)
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(in) :: G_state
            integer,intent(in) :: num_threads
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(out) :: x
        end subroutine xc_to_x

    end module precon

    end interface
end python module mwe_f2py
```
This compiles into a python module `mwe_f2py.cpython-311-x86_64-linux-gnu.so` with the following command under python 3.11.6,
```
f2py --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem -c mwe_f2py.pyf --opt='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
However, with the new-fangled meson build system under python 3.12.4, the following command complains about undefined reference to omp_set_num_threads,
```
CFLAGS='-qopenmp' FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c mwe_f2py.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
So either there's a way to specify flags I'm not getting, or `f2py` got broken.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2278950094/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I have compiled python 3.12.4 from source on a computing cluster, and I am trying to compile numpy. The goal is to install it in the same tree as the locally compiled python binary. We have intel compilers on the cluster, so I am trying to get numpy to use intel MKL libraries. I compiled numpy with
```
CC=icx CXX=icpx python -m pip install --no-build-isolation -v . -Cbuild-dir=build -Csetup-args=-Dallow-noblas=false -Csetup-args=-Dblas-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dlapack-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dblas=mkl-static-lp64-iomp -Csetup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-static-lp64-iomp

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-08-09T19:37:02Z","updated_at":"2024-08-11T18:32:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I have compiled python 3.12.4 and numpy 2.0.1 with intel oneapi 2024.1 compilers on a compute cluster for multiple people to use. I have some f2py code that explicitly uses openmp library calls such as `omp_set_num_threads`. With previous versions of python, including 3.11.6, I could compile this with 
```
f2py --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem -c mwe_f2py.pyf --opt='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
which would produce a perfectly working python module `mwe_f2py.cpython-311-x86_64-linux-gnu.so`. However, since everything has moved to the new-fangled meson now, this is no longer possible on python 3.12.4. So instead if I do
```
CFLAGS='-qopenmp' FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c mwe_f2py.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
it raises the error `undefined reference to 'omp_set_num_threads'`. This should not happen because I am supplying `-qopenmp` as a compiler option.

### Reproduce the code example:
Put the following in `mwe_f2py.F90`
```fortran
module precon

implicit none

public :: xc_to_x

contains

subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)

   use omp_lib

   integer, intent(in)             :: n_state, num_threads
   double precision, intent(in)    :: G_state(n_state)
   double precision, intent(out)   :: x(n_state)
   integer                         :: i

   x = 0.0

   call omp_set_num_threads(num_threads)

   !$omp parallel do schedule(guided)
   do i = 1, n_state
      x(i) = G_state(i) * G_state(n_state-i+1)
   end do
   !$omp end parallel do

end subroutine xc_to_x

end module precon
```
and the following in `mwe_f2py.pyf`,
```fortran
python module mwe_f2py
    interface

    module precon

        subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)
            integer,optional,intent(in),check(len(G_state)==n_state),depend(G_state) :: n_state=len(G_state)
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(in) :: G_state
            integer,intent(in) :: num_threads
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(out) :: x
        end subroutine xc_to_x

    end module precon

    end interface
end python module mwe_f2py
```

SciPy or fastmatrix code is not the culprit here.

OK, I upgraded to intel oneapi 2024.2, which got rid of that error. It's apparently a known bug with older (pre-2024) intel oneapi compilers, which [need a specific version of gcc/g++](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/troubleshooting/error-c-header-file-not-found-with-dpc-c-compiler.html). So now it's back to the error related to redefined `I`, specifically:

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-08-09T19:37:02Z","updated_at":"2024-08-19T19:39:12Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I have compiled python 3.12.4 and numpy 2.0.1 with intel oneapi 2024.1 compilers on a compute cluster for multiple people to use. I have some f2py code that explicitly uses openmp library calls such as `omp_set_num_threads`. With previous versions of python, including 3.11.6, I could compile this with 
```
f2py --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem -c mwe_f2py.pyf --opt='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
which would produce a perfectly working python module `mwe_f2py.cpython-311-x86_64-linux-gnu.so`. However, since everything has moved to the new-fangled meson now, this is no longer possible on python 3.12.4. So instead if I do
```
CFLAGS='-qopenmp' FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c mwe_f2py.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
it raises the error `undefined reference to 'omp_set_num_threads'`. This should not happen because I am supplying `-qopenmp` as a compiler option.

### Reproduce the code example:
Put the following in `mwe_f2py.F90`
```fortran
module precon

implicit none

public :: xc_to_x

contains

subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)

   use omp_lib

   integer, intent(in)             :: n_state, num_threads
   double precision, intent(in)    :: G_state(n_state)
   double precision, intent(out)   :: x(n_state)
   integer                         :: i

   x = 0.0

   call omp_set_num_threads(num_threads)

   !$omp parallel do schedule(guided)
   do i = 1, n_state
      x(i) = G_state(i) * G_state(n_state-i+1)
   end do
   !$omp end parallel do

end subroutine xc_to_x

end module precon
```
and the following in `mwe_f2py.pyf`,
```fortran
python module mwe_f2py
    interface

    module precon

        subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)
            integer,optional,intent(in),check(len(G_state)==n_state),depend(G_state) :: n_state=len(G_state)
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(in) :: G_state
            integer,intent(in) :: num_threads
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(out) :: x
        end subroutine xc_to_x

    end module precon

    end interface
end python module mwe_f2py
```

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":16917452,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNjkxNzQ1Mg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/component:%20numpy.f2py","name":"component: numpy.f2py","color":"009800","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-08-09T19:37:02Z","updated_at":"2024-08-21T13:53:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I have compiled python 3.12.4 and numpy 2.0.1 with intel oneapi 2024.1 compilers on a compute cluster for multiple people to use. I have some f2py code that explicitly uses openmp library calls such as `omp_set_num_threads`. With previous versions of python, including 3.11.6, I could compile this with 
```
f2py --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem -c mwe_f2py.pyf --opt='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
which would produce a perfectly working python module `mwe_f2py.cpython-311-x86_64-linux-gnu.so`. However, since everything has moved to the new-fangled meson now, this is no longer possible on python 3.12.4. So instead if I do
```
CFLAGS='-qopenmp' FC=ifx CC=icx CXX=icpx f2py -c mwe_f2py.pyf --f77flags='-qopenmp' --f90flags='-qopenmp' -liomp5 mwe_f2py.F90
```
it raises the error `undefined reference to 'omp_set_num_threads'`. This should not happen because I am supplying `-qopenmp` as a compiler option.

### Reproduce the code example:
Put the following in `mwe_f2py.F90`
```fortran
module precon

implicit none

public :: xc_to_x

contains

subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)

   use omp_lib

   integer, intent(in)             :: n_state, num_threads
   double precision, intent(in)    :: G_state(n_state)
   double precision, intent(out)   :: x(n_state)
   integer                         :: i

   x = 0.0

   call omp_set_num_threads(num_threads)

   !$omp parallel do schedule(guided)
   do i = 1, n_state
      x(i) = G_state(i) * G_state(n_state-i+1)
   end do
   !$omp end parallel do

end subroutine xc_to_x

end module precon
```
and the following in `mwe_f2py.pyf`,
```fortran
python module mwe_f2py
    interface

    module precon

        subroutine xc_to_x(n_state, G_state, num_threads, x)
            integer,optional,intent(in),check(len(G_state)==n_state),depend(G_state) :: n_state=len(G_state)
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(in) :: G_state
            integer,intent(in) :: num_threads
            double precision,dimension(n_state),intent(out) :: x
        end subroutine xc_to_x

    end module precon

    end interface
end python module mwe_f2py
```

Describe your issue.

I am installing scipy from source on an HPC on top of python 3.11.6. The code builds without errors, but `scipy.test()` segfaults at `scipy/sparse/linalg/_eigen/arpack/tests/test_arpack.py`. We have intel MKL on the HPC, so I built scipy with 
```
CC=icx CXX=icpx FC=ifx python dev.py build -C-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -C-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
python dev.py test -s sparse
```

As you correctly surmised, I have since figured out that my initial complain of not finding MKL at runtime was because I was loading the wrong numpy, i.e., not the numpy that had successfully compiled against MKL. This in turn was because I was expecting `spin build` to install into python's "global" prefix, i.e., the folder tree where the python binary was located, and not to `build-install` inside the numpy source tree. Why do/did I use `spin build`? Because I find meson build [frustrating](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26197) when debugging build errors; it rarely tells me what actually went wrong, and it always points to a non-existent log file, like so:
```
A full log can be found at /tmp/.../build/meson-logs/meson-log.txt
```
I cannot tell you how many hours I've spent hunting down these supposedly helpful meson log files that don't exist. I've also found that `pip install` takes me down that same logfile-hunting journey when a build fails, probably because it uses `meson build`. That's why I use `spin build`, at least I can figure out from the output what went wrong. This is not my complain alone, a Google search of "missing meson log file" yields several discussion posts such as [this](https://www.reddit.com/r/learnpython/comments/12vfgod/how_to_keep_mesonlog_files/).

Anyway, my current situation is

- I have MKL installed in a global location `/opt/intel/...`
- I have built python from source and installed it in a custom prefix `/opt/local/.../python/3.11.6` which I want multiple users to be able to use. This python is built with Intel compilers.
- I don't have a mechanism to *prevent* users from installing packages into their own `PYTHONPATH` and loading (say) the wrong `numpy` and `scipy`, but it's understood that if they do that they're on their own. My responsibility is to provide a python+numpy+scipy that works system-wide and uses the MKL that's installed in `/opt/intel`.
- I have finally figured out how to build numpy to use the MKL on our system,
```
export PKG_CONFIG_PATH=/opt/intel/oneapi/mkl/2022.1.0/lib/pkgconfig:$PKG_CONFIG_PATH
CC=icx CXX=icpx spin build -- -Dallow-noblas=false -Dblas-order=mkl -Dlapack-order=mkl -Dblas=mkl-static-lp64-iomp -Dlapack=mkl-static-lp64-iomp
```
- My trouble now comes when I try to make this `numpy` build available to all users by putting it in `/opt/local/.../python/3.11.6`, because the above step only installs into `build-install`. Right now I `rsync` from `build-install` into the location I want. That can't be the recommended way.

I recently installed `numpy 1.26.4` from source on a machine that has intel oneapi compilers (icx/icpx) and MKL libraries. I installed with
```
export PKG_CONFIG_PATH=/ford1/local/intel/oneapi/mkl/2022.1.0/lib/pkgconfig:$PKG_CONFIG_PATH # this is where my .pc files are
CC=icx CXX=icpx spin build -- -Dallow-noblas=false -Dblas-order=mkl,openblas -Dlapack-order=mkl,openblas -Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp -Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-iomp
```
and during compilation, it told me that it had found the correct shared library,
```
Test features "SSE SSE2 SSE3" : Supported
Test features "SSSE3" : Supported
Test features "SSE41" : Supported
Test features "POPCNT" : Supported

Configuring npy_cpu_dispatch_config.h using configuration
Message:
CPU Optimization Options
  baseline:
    Requested : min
    Enabled   : SSE SSE2 SSE3

After ensuring that `mkl-static-lp64-iomp.pc` was in my `PKG_CONFIG_PATH`, I issued the following:
```
CC=icx CXX=icpx python -m pip install --no-build-isolation -v . -Cbuild-dir=build -Csetup-args=-Dallow-noblas=false -Csetup-args=-Dblas-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dlapack-order=mkl -Csetup-args=-Dblas=mkl-static-lp64-iomp -Csetup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-static-lp64-iomp
```
which successfully compiled numpy and installed it in the same folder tree/prefix when my python binary is located. Yay, thanks @rgommers! Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```

### Context for the issue:

`np.any()` on an array of `False` elements returning `False` is a pretty basic functionality of numpy, and behaves as expected in multiple other versions of `numpy` across platforms. I rely on that to filter data regularly. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```

Describe the issue:

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```


### Error message:

_No response_

```
import sys; print(sys.version)
3.11.6 (main, Dec 11 2023, 15:56:58) [GCC Intel(R) C++ gcc 11.3.1 mode]
print(np.__version__)
1.26.2
```

I am using icpx (Intel(R) oneAPI DPC++/C++ Compiler 2024.1.0 (2024.1.0.20240308)) and keep seeing a missing symbol error:

I have found that if I build with `icx` and `icpx`, the resulting numpy library has none of the architecture-optimized instruction sets, even when compiled with `-xHost`. So I've fallen back on compiling with `icc`, which is available in the intel oneapi as well. Here are the steps:

1. Edit `site.cfg` to make sure the MKL include and library folders are set correctly. For me they are
```
[mkl]
library_dirs = /apps/spack-managed/oneapi-2022.2.1/intel-oneapi-mkl-2022.2.1-klrcilzymbsllrr6wmepfg2cfzem5ekd/mkl/2022.2.1/lib/intel64
include_dirs = /apps/spack-managed/oneapi-2022.2.1/intel-oneapi-mkl-2022.2.1-klrcilzymbsllrr6wmepfg2cfzem5ekd/mkl/2022.2.1/include
mkl_libs = mkl_rt
lapack_libs =
```
2. Check `numpy/distutils/intelccompiler.py` and `numpy/distutils/fcompiler/intel.py` to make sure that `icc` and `ifort` are being used. Also check the optimizer flags. I have `-fPIC -fp-model strict -O3 -diag-disable=10441 -fomit-frame-pointer -qopenmp`. The `diag-disable` flag is to stop the compiler from reminding me to use `icx` instead of `icc`. I tried `-xHost` as well, but that includes `AVX512_SKX` in the base optimizations, which means it can no longer be disabled at runtime.
3. Compile and install with `python setup.py config --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem build_clib --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem build_ext --compiler=intelem --fcompiler=intelem install`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2037912586/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe the issue:

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```


### Error message:

_No response_

```
import sys; print(sys.version)
3.11.6 (main, Dec 11 2023, 15:56:58) [GCC Intel(R) C++ gcc 11.3.1 mode]
print(np.__version__)
1.26.2
```

Describe the issue:

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```

_No response_

```
import sys; print(sys.version)

```

Describe the issue:

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

```
import sys; print(sys.version)
3.11.6 (main, Dec 11 2023, 15:56:58) [GCC Intel(R) C++ gcc 11.3.1 mode]
print(np.__version__)
1.26.2
```

17:55:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

`np.any` on a boolean array where all elements are `False` should return `False`. This has been the case for the past decade or so, across numerous `numpy` versions. However, I recently installed `numpy 1.26.2`, and that is not working as expected. For a 1D boolean array of 63 or less elements, all `False`, `np.any` returns `False`. However, if the length is 64 or more, `np.any` returns True.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
Y = np.zeros(63, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "False", as expected
Y = np.zeros(64, bool) ; print(np.any(Y)) # prints "True"
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

```
import sys; print(sys.version)
3.11.6 (main, Dec 11 2023, 15:56:58) [GCC Intel(R) C++ gcc 11.3.1 mode]
print(np.__version__)
1.26.2
```

However, it is slower. It seems that I can either 

1. Compile with `icx -fPIC -fp-model strict -xHost -O3 -fomit-frame-pointer -fiopenmp` and get a version of numpy that is aware of all AVX512 instructions, and does not have the problem in the original bug report. However, performing the timing test [above](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26197#issuecomment-2034880623), it is slow and takes ~9 seconds.

2. Compile with `icc -fPIC -fp-model strict -xHost -O3 -fomit-frame-pointer -qopenmp` and get a version of numpy that is also aware of AVX512 instructions, but *fails* the original test. It is, however, faster in the timing test, taking ~2 seconds.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2052218092/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

However, I've noticed that the font smoothing, at least for plots displayed on the screen, is noticeably poorer. The code below offers a MWE:
```python
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
plt.plot(np.linspace(0,1,10), np.linspace(0,1,10), '-o')
plt.setp(plt.gca().get_xticklabels(), size=14)
plt.setp(plt.gca().get_yticklabels(), size=14)
```

With the `mplcairo` backend, the tick labels look _jagged_ and not smooth, as below.
![Screenshot 2023-12-28 at 4 32 03 PM](https://github.com/matplotlib/mplcairo/assets/3866945/fceaa9b9-1f77-47d1-a415-dfec4cd043d5)

Everything is identical for the production of the two figures except the backend, which I switch in `.matplotlibrc`. Am I missing something? Is there a way to get `mplcairo` to render fonts with proper smoothing?

However, I've noticed that the font smoothing, at least for plots displayed on the screen, is noticeably poorer. The code below offers a MWE:
```python
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

fig = plt.figure()
plt.plot(np.linspace(0,1,10), np.linspace(0,1,10), '-o')
plt.setp(plt.gca().get_xticklabels(), size=14)
plt.setp(plt.gca().get_yticklabels(), size=14)
```

With the `mplcairo` backend, the tick labels look _jagged_ and not smooth, as below.
![Screenshot 2023-12-28 at 4 32 03 PM](https://github.com/matplotlib/mplcairo/assets/3866945/fceaa9b9-1f77-47d1-a415-dfec4cd043d5)
Whereas with the `MacOSX` backend gives me noticeably smoother fonts.
![Screenshot 2023-12-28 at 4 33 00 PM](https://github.com/matplotlib/mplcairo/assets/3866945/d20e8d94-6249-4fb3-80f2-355a91bdc8d4)

Everything is identical for the production of the two figures except the backend, which I switch in `.matplotlibrc`. Am I missing something? Is there a way to get `mplcairo` to render fonts with proper smoothing?

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

Describe your issue.

I am trying to build `scipy` 1.11.4 from source with `meson` because I'd like to take advantage of Intel MKL linear algebra libraries that are installed on our system. I followed instructions [here](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/16200#issuecomment-1615094519) for how to link with MKL libraries while building with `meson`. The build failed after some steps, and scrolling through the output the only errors I could see had to do with an unknown option `-fvisibility` passed to the fortran compiler. I'm using Intel's C/C++ and fortran compilers, `icx` and `ifx`.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
pip3 install . --config-settings=setup-args=-Dblas=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq --config-settings=setup-args=-Dlapack=mkl-dynamic-lp64-seq
```

I have some 2D fields that I would like to plot with matplotlib's pcolormesh. If I use a colormap without any transparency, all works as it should. However, if I use a colormap with transparency, I get diagonal lines across the face of each grid cell. Here's a minimal working example, minus the code needed to create a colormap with transparency.

### Code for reproduction

```python
import numpy as np
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

X = np.linspace(-3.,3.,100)
Y = np.linspace(-3.,3.,100)
XX, YY = np.meshgrid(X,Y)
ZZ = XX**2 + YY**2
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))
plt.pcolormesh(X, Y, ZZ, cmap=plt.cm.viridis, edgecolors='none', shading='gouraud') # without transparency

my_cmap = make_transparent(plt.cm.viridis) 
# Code of above is not important, I think. Basically makes the alpha of the left edge 0, 
# and saturates at a maximum alpha of 0.8 at the right. After this transformation,
# my_cmap(0.0) gives (0.267004, 0.004874, 0.329415, 0.0), while my_cmap(1.0) gives
# (0.993248, 0.906157, 0.143936, 0.8)
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6,6))
plt.pcolormesh(X, Y, ZZ, cmap=my_cmap, edgecolors='none', shading='gouraud')
```


### Actual outcome

When `pcolormesh` is used with a colormap without transparency, [all works as it should](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1DhSoMIL9iyMARmxS8dxxAbZN9ifXuOsC/view?usp=sharing). However, with a colormap with transparency, [thin lines are drawn](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1jZ-8zom1KpMi1qi5Ijro51psZfP0W0Hd/view?usp=sharing) on the plot. This is more obvious if I manually [zoom in](https://drive.google.com/file/d/14lOhzA6wXmyn2YJi48Sd0QWqliydtjKi/view?usp=sharing).

### Expected outcome

I expect `pcolormesh` to not produce additional lines. This is especially troublesome when I use `pcolormesh` in `cartopy` to plot 2D fields on top of a map, which is when I first noticed this artifact.

Diagonal and box lines are produced by `pcolormesh` using a cmap with transparency.


### Code for reproduction

```python
import matplotlib.colors as m_colors
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
plt.pcolormesh(np.array([[0, 1, 2], [0, 1, 2]]),
               np.array([[0, 0, 0], [1, 1, 1]]),
               np.array([[0, 1, 2], [0, 1, 2]]),
               cmap=m_colors.LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
                   "", [(0, 0, 0, 0), (0, 0, 0, 1)]),
               shading="gouraud")
plt.plot()
```

I use `LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list` to create custom colormaps. A very simple one, which just assigns two RGB values to the two end points, does not work any more after update to matplotlib 3.8.0.

### Code for reproduction

```python
from matplotlib import colors
val_col =  [(0.0, (0.95, 0.0, 0.0)), (1.0, (0.95, 0.95, 0.95))]
cm = colors.LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list('test', val_col)
```


### Actual outcome

```
File ~/packages/macports/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/matplotlib/colors.py:1067, in LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(name, colors, N, gamma)
   1063     vals = np.linspace(0, 1, len(colors))
   1065 r, g, b, a = to_rgba_array(colors).T
   1066 cdict = {
-> 1067     "red": np.column_stack([vals, r, r]),
   1068     "green": np.column_stack([vals, g, g]),
   1069     "blue": np.column_stack([vals, b, b]),
   1070     "alpha": np.column_stack([vals, a, a]),
   1071 }
   1073 return LinearSegmentedColormap(name, cdict, N, gamma)

File ~/packages/macports/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.11/lib/python3.11/site-packages/numpy/lib/shape_base.py:652, in column_stack(tup)
    650         arr = array(arr, copy=False, subok=True, ndmin=2).T
    651     arrays.append(arr)
--> 652 return _nx.concatenate(arrays, 1)

ValueError: all the input array dimensions except for the concatenation axis must match exactly, but along dimension 0, the array at index 0 has size 2 and the array at index 1 has size 3
```

### Expected outcome

This should construct a new colormap going smoothly from red to white. This used to work until I recently updated matplotlib to 3.8.0.

### Additional information

The issue seems to be that the routine `to_rgba_array` takes a length-2 object and returns r, g and b that are all length 3. Specifically, the line

```python
r, g, b, a = to_rgba_array(colors).T
```

takes `colors`, which is `((0.95, 0.0, 0.0), (0.95, 0.95, 0.95))`, and returns `r` as `[0.95 0.95 0.95]`, and so on. It's obvious that `np.column_stack([vals, r, r])` should fail in that case, because `vals` is length 2, and `r` is length 3. This used to work in an earlier version of matplotlib, so I suppose `to_rgba_array` has changed recently.

### Operating system

OS/X

### Matplotlib Version

3.8.0

### Matplotlib Backend

MacOSX

### Python version

3.11.6

    651     arrays.append(arr)
--> 652 return _nx.concatenate(arrays, 1)

ValueError: all the input array dimensions except for the concatenation axis must match exactly, but along dimension 0, the array at index 0 has size 2 and the array at index 1 has size 3
```

### Expected outcome

This should construct a new colormap going smoothly from red to white. This used to work until I recently updated matplotlib to 3.8.0.

### Additional information

The issue seems to be that the routine `to_rgba_array` takes a length-2 object and returns r, g and b that are all length 3. Specifically, the line

```python
r, g, b, a = to_rgba_array(colors).T
```

takes `colors`, which is `((0.95, 0.0, 0.0), (0.95, 0.95, 0.95))`, and returns `r` as `[0.95 0.95 0.95]`, and so on. It's obvious that `np.column_stack([vals, r, r])` should fail in that case, because `vals` is length 2, and `r` is length 3. This used to work in an earlier version of matplotlib, so I suppose `to_rgba_array` has changed recently.

```python
from matplotlib import colors
val_col =  [(0.0, (0.95, 0.0, 0.0)), (1.0, (0.95, 0.95, 0.95))]
cm = colors.LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list('test', val_col)
```

    651     arrays.append(arr)
--> 652 return _nx.concatenate(arrays, 1)

ValueError: all the input array dimensions except for the concatenation axis must match exactly, but along dimension 0, the array at index 0 has size 2 and the array at index 1 has size 3
```

### Expected outcome

This should construct a new colormap going smoothly from red to white. This used to work until I recently updated matplotlib to 3.8.0.

### Additional information

```python

```

takes `colors`, which is `((0.95, 0.0, 0.0), (0.95, 0.95, 0.95))`, and returns `r` as `[0.95 0.95 0.95]`, and so on. I am trying to plot some data on a map at high resolution, and I think I've come across a limit on the size of the image `pyplot.savefig` can save. When I save a 21600x10800 image, it works. However, when I try to save 43200x21600 image, it only saves half the image, always exactly half. According to my calculation, a 43200x21600 RGB image without any compression should occupy 2.6 GB, which is large but well within the 32 GB memory of my computer. I would like to know if a size limit is encoded somewhere for `savefig`, and if there's a way to bypass it. The image displays fine on my screen, just doesn't save to PNG.

### Code for reproduction

```python
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# make a checkerboard array of +1 and -1 for plotting
A = np.zeros((90,180))
for i in range(90):
    for j in range(180):
        A[i,j] = (-1)**i * (-1)**j

# create a large canvas
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(24,12))
ax = plt.axes([0.,0.,1.,1.])
ax.axis('off')

# plot and save
ax.imshow(A, vmin=-1, vmax=1, cmap=plt.cm.coolwarm, interpolation='none')
plt.savefig('image_43200.png', dpi=1800) # 24x12 inch image at 1800 dpi = 43200x21600
```


### Actual outcome

No matter what I plot, `savefig` saves exactly half the image, always the left half. [This](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Il4VBD9tlvbjs8BJiHhgJyf2d-Yivgus/view?usp=sharing) is what the image looks like opened in GIMP.

### Expected outcome

On screen, [this](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1liyTCvsqyCmydEDKS4nKkLwM7r6vQ8G2/view?usp=sharing) is what the image looks like, which is correct. I would expect the saved image to look like this as well.

### Additional information

Bug summary

I am trying to plot some data on a map at high resolution, and I think I've come across a limit on the size of the image `pyplot.savefig` can save. When I save a 21600x10800 image, it works. However, when I try to save 43200x21600 image, it only saves half the image, always exactly half. According to my calculation, a 43200x21600 RGB image without any compression should occupy 2.6 GB, which is large but well within the 32 GB memory of my computer. I would like to know if a size limit is encoded somewhere for `savefig`, and if there's a way to bypass it. The image displays fine on my screen, just doesn't save to PNG.

### Code for reproduction

```python
from matplotlib import pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# make a checkerboard array of +1 and -1 for plotting
A = np.zeros((90,180))
for i in range(90):
    for j in range(180):
        A[i,j] = (-1)**i * (-1)**j

# create a large canvas
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(24,12))
ax = plt.axes([0.,0.,1.,1.])
ax.axis('off')

# plot and save
ax.imshow(A, vmin=-1, vmax=1, cmap=plt.cm.coolwarm, interpolation='none')
plt.savefig('image_43200.png', dpi=1800) # 24x12 inch image at 1800 dpi = 43200x21600
```


### Actual outcome

No matter what I plot, `savefig` saves exactly half the image, always the left half. [This](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Il4VBD9tlvbjs8BJiHhgJyf2d-Yivgus/view?usp=sharing) is what the image looks like opened in GIMP.

### Expected outcome

On screen, [this](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1liyTCvsqyCmydEDKS4nKkLwM7r6vQ8G2/view?usp=sharing) is what the image looks like, which is correct. I would expect the saved image to look like this as well.

### Additional information

MacOS Ventura 13.5.2

### Matplotlib Version

MacOSX
